pattern: Because only a bounded number of cells are blocked, they can enclose at most a fixed-size pocket, so a flood fill that gives up once it visits more than that many cells decides reachability without touching the full grid.
hints: The grid is astronomically large, but the blockers are few; how big an area can a handful of walls actually fence off?
With b blockers the largest sealed region they can form is bounded by about b*(b-1)/2 cells, so a BFS that escapes that many cells must be free.
Flood fill from the source and from the target separately; each search succeeds if it reaches the other endpoint or visits more than the enclosable-area bound before running out of frontier.

edge_inputs.description: source boxed into a corner by two walls
no walls, target far away is reachable
source enclosed by a small diagonal wall, target outside
walls present but never enclose anything
source against the grid edge, escapes along it
target itself sealed in a tiny pocket

stress.description: random sparse walls over a small window so enclosures sometimes form
stress.note: small window keeps both the optimal bound-BFS and the compression oracle cheap and correct
